extracted_snippet: A.1 Introduction This Annex details the hazard mitigation planning elements specific to the City of Fremont, a previously participating jurisdiction to the 2016 City of Fremont Local Hazard Mitigation Plan (LHMP or Plan). This Annex is not intended to be a standalone document but appends to and supplements the information contained in the Base Plan document. As such, all sections of the Base Plan, including the planning process and other procedural requirements apply to and were met by the City. This Annex provides additional information specific to Fremont, with a focus on providing additional details on the risk assessment and mitigation strategy for this jurisdiction.

extraction_reason: PDF source text contained a planning keyword but no configured hazard alias.
extracted_snippet: Tri-City City of Fremont Annex A-2 Local Hazard Mitigation Plan Update April 2024 A.2 Planning Process The City participated in the common planning process with all of the other jurisdictions to create the Tri- City Planning Committee. This is discussed in detail in Section 3.2.1 of the Base Plan. In addition to providing representation on the Tri-City Planning Committee, City of Fremont personnel formulated their own internal planning team to support the broader planning process requirements. Table A-1 lists City of Fremont employees who contributed to the project, their positions, and how they participated in the planning process. Additional details on Plan participation and City representatives are included in Appendix A. The City noted that the information regarding plan contributors in this section and in Appendix A is by no means exhaustive. Source: City of Fremont Coordination with other community planning efforts is paramount to the successful implementation of this LHMP Update. This section provides information on how the City integrated the previously FEMA approved 2016 Plan into existing planning mechanisms and programs. Specifically, the City incorporated into or implemented the 2016 LHMP through other plans and programs shown in Table A-2.

extraction_reason: PDF source text contained configured alias “sea level rise”.
extracted_snippet: Tri-City City of Fremont Annex A-5 Local Hazard Mitigation Plan Update April 2024 Table A-2 City of Fremont 2016 LHMP Incorporation and Integration Planning Mechanism Previous LHMP Was Incorporated or Implemented Through Details City of Fremont – General Plan (Adopted December 2011) The General Plan’s Safety Element addresses the following, and other topics: • Seismic and Geologic Hazards • Flood Hazards • Fire Hazards • Hazardous Materials and Waste • Community Emergency Preparedness Policy 10-5-5: Local Hazard Mitigation Plan (p. 10-60) states that Fremont will participate in regional disaster planning initiatives and maintain and update the City’s Local Hazard Mitigation Plan every 5 years, as prescribed by FEMA. By the language above, the 2016 Plan was adopted by reference into the 2011 General Plan. Additionally, the General Plan’s Public Facilities Element describes City-owned and other public facilities, and also addresses utility services and providers – and flood control. City of Fremont – Emergency Operations Plan, Basic Plan (2020) The Emergency Operations Plan (EOP) provides an analysis of local hazards and references the LHMP. City of Fremont – Climate Action Plan (CAP): Climate Ready Fremont: Fremont’s pathway to a low-carbon, sustainable, and resilient future (2023, pending adoption) The CAP implemented portions of the LHMP. From the CAP, p. 1-6: Fremont’s LHMP identifies and assesses the risk of three hazards caused by climate change - drought, extreme heat, and sea level rise. The LHMP includes strategies to address these identified risks. Capital Improvement Program budget Every two years, the City adopts a five-year capital budget, known as the Capital Improvement Program budget (CIP). The CIP funds public infrastructure projects including street repair, traffic improvements, civic facility construction and maintenance, and park development and maintenance. Example mitigation projects implemented through the CIP in the past five years include emergency generator installation and maintenance.

extracted_snippet: Source: Annual Comprehensive Financial Report for the Fiscal Year Ended June 30, 2023 A.4 Risk Assessment As defined by the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA), risk is a combination of hazard, vulnerability, and exposure. “It is the impact that a hazard would have on people, services, facilities, and structures in a community and refers to the likelihood of a hazard event resulting in an adverse condition that causes injury or damage.” The Risk Assessment in Section A.4 identifies and profiles relevant hazards to which Fremont is vulnerable, and assesses the exposure of lives, property, infrastructure, and the environment to these hazards. The Risk Assessment allows for a better understanding of natural hazard risk and provides a framework for developing and prioritizing mitigation actions to reduce risk from future hazard events. The Risk Assessment includes the following sections: ➢ A.4.1 Assets Inventory and Growth and Development Trends ➢ A.4.2 Hazard Identification ➢ A.4.3 Hazard Profiles and Vulnerability to Specific Hazards

extracted_snippet: Source: CDC Social Vulnerability Index – map retrieved 2/26/2024 Level of Vulnerability Rating: Faint Purple – Low; Light Purple – Low/Medium; Blue – Medium/High; Dark Blue – High; Grey Hatched – No Data; Grey – Not Available CA DWR Disadvantaged Community Mapping CA DWR has developed a web-based application to assist local agencies and other interested parties in evaluating disadvantaged community (DAC) status throughout the State, using the definition provided by Proposition 84 Integrated Regional Water Management (IRWM) Guidelines (2016). A statewide Census Tract GIS Tiger file is used to determine if a census tract is DAC or not by adding American Community Survey Median Household Income data at the census tract level. Only those census geographies that meet the DAC definition are shown on the map (i.e., only those with an annual median household income (MHI) that is less than 80 percent of the Statewide annual MHI (PRC Section 75005(g)). In addition, those census geographies having an annual MHI that is less than 60 percent of the Statewide annual MHI are shown as “Severely Disadvantaged Communities” (SDAC). The DAC Mapping Tool is an interactive map application that allows users to overlay the following three US Census geographies as separate data layers: Census Place, Census Tract, and Census Block Group. Figure A-10 illustrates DACs in Fremont. Identified DACs are located in the Irvington neighborhood and in the north of Fremont.

extracted_snippet: Source: CA DWR – map retrieved 10/03/2023 FEMA Community Disaster Resilience Zones FEMA’s Community Disaster Resilience Zones aim to build and strengthen community resilience across the nation by driving federal, public, and private resources to the most at-risk and in-need communities. The 2022 Community Disaster Resilience Zones Act uses FEMA’s National Risk Index to identify the most at-risk and in-need communities to identify resilience zones. Designated zones will be prioritized for targeted federal support, such as increased cost-share for resilience and mitigation projects, lessening the financial burden on communities to perform resilience-related activities. On September 6, 2023, FEMA announced the initial 483 designations in all 50 states and the District of Columbia. Figure A-11 illustrates Community Disaster Resilience Zones in Fremont in teal green.

extracted_snippet: Source: FEMA. Map retrieved 10/4/2023. City Planning Team Input In recent years, Fremont and the entire Bay Area region have experienced a significant growth in homelessness. During this time unsheltered homelessness in Fremont has grown significantly, outpacing available local resources such as shelter, housing options, and abatement services. Part of the City's homeless response includes addressing encampments throughout Fremont. Efforts include scheduling cleanup activities, administering guidelines, and overseeing relocation efforts. It is important to note that these efforts can be restricted by legal limitations and a lack of affordable housing. The City noted that vulnerable population includes both housed and un-housed individuals at all ages and income levels. While it is assumed that those at a higher income level may not need as much assistance, they may still need help (i.e., due to age, physical impairment, non-English speaking, etc.) than those not considered to be vulnerable. The California Department of Education’s California School for the Deaf and California School for the Blind are statewide educational resources located in Fremont, collectively serving approximately 400 students.

Wetlands in the Tri-City Planning Area are shown in Section 4.2.1 and detailed for the City of Fremont in Table A-13. Table A-13 City of Fremont – Wetlands Types and Acreages Wetlands Type Acres Freshwater Emergent Wetland 889 Freshwater Forested/Shrub Wetland 2 Freshwater Pond 303 Lake 8,592 Riverine 640 Fremont Total 10,426 Source: US Fish and Wildlife Service Historic and Cultural Resources Historic and cultural resources are difficult to replace and add character and sense of place to a community. Should a natural disaster occur, historic and cultural properties and locations can be at risk. The City of Fremont has a stock of historically significant homes, public buildings, and landmarks. The California Department of Parks and Recreation Office of Historic Preservation (OHP) was the primary source of information. OHP administers the National Register of Historic Places, the California Register of Historical Resources, California Historical Landmarks, and the California Points of Historical Interest programs. Each program has different eligibility criteria and procedural requirements. These requirements are detailed in Section 4.2.1 of the Base Plan. Table A-14 lists the historic buildings in the City. extracted_snippet: Source: Photo courtesy of Khanh Vo. It should be noted that these lists may not be complete, as they may not include those currently in the nomination process and not yet listed. Additionally, as defined by the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) and the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA), any property over 50 years of age is considered a historic resource and is potentially eligible for the National Register. Thus, in the event that the property is to be altered, or has been altered, as the result of a major federal action, the property must be evaluated under the guidelines set forth by CEQA and NEPA. Structural mitigation projects are considered alterations for the purpose of this regulation. City Planning Team Natural, Historic, and Cultural Resources In addition to the resources listed in the sections above, the City Planning Team noted the following mix of natural, historic, and cultural resources: ➢ Lake Elizabeth (located in Fremont Central Park) ➢ California Nursery Historical Park – owned and maintained by the City of Fremont. ➢ Centerville Train Station – owned and maintained by the City of Fremont. The Fremont ACE station, also known as the Centerville Depot, appears now as it did in 1910 as a historical Southern Pacific station. ➢ Chadbourne Carriage House – owned and maintained by the City of Fremont. ➢ Durham House - owned and maintained by the City of Fremont. ➢ Harvey Cabin – owned and maintained by the City of Fremont. ➢ Rancho Higuera Historical Park – owned and maintained by the City of Fremont. The Museum of Local History, an independent organization, offers tours, and operates a nearby museum. The historical park is located near Mission San Jose, one of five towns that incorporated to become the City of Fremont in 1956. ➢ Museum of Local History – owned by the City of Fremont. The building was quarters to Fremont Fire Department’s Engine Company 4. https://museumoflocalhistory.org/contact/calendar-special-events/.

extracted_snippet: Tri-City City of Fremont Annex A-40 Local Hazard Mitigation Plan Update April 2024 Property Use 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 Total Square Footage[1] 679,309 2,609,965 67,869 304,920 131,800 405,445 4,199,308 Source: City of Fremont *The non-residential properties vary from single buildings to multi-family housing. The City noted that very few of these properties are built in the 1% annual chance floodplain - 0 2017, 3 in 2018, 6 in 2019, 0 in 2020, 0 in 2021, and 0 in 2022. All building in the floodplain is subject to the City of Fremont Floodplain Ordinance. The Community Development Department (CDD) oversees planning and permitting for all new developments. As part of the permitting process, CDD personnel review projects according to established criteria, and using the City’s building and permitting software system, Accela. Each permit is reviewed to determine whether the proposed development project is located in any of the following hazard areas: ➢ Alquist-Priolo Fault Trace Zone ➢ Earthquake-induced Landslide Zone ➢ Earthquake-induced Liquefaction Zone ➢ FEMA NFIP Special Flood Hazard Area ➢ FEMA NFIP Flood Zone ➢ Current FIRM panels ➢ Very High Fire Hazard Severity Zone2 To determine whether a development is located in one of the above areas, staff use the City’s Enterprise GIS system, as well as California Geological Survey (CGS) interactive mapping. Figure A-15 and Figure A-16 illustrate hazard layers in the City’s Enterprise GIS system. Figure A-17 provides a sample of how hazard exposure is recorded in Accela.

Likely: Between 10 and 100% chance of occurrence in next year or has a recurrence interval of 10 years or less. Occasional: Between 1 and 10% chance of occurrence in the next year or has a recurrence interval of 11 to 100 years. Unlikely: Less than 1% chance of occurrence in next 100 years or has a recurrence interval of greater than every 100 years.
